items.title: UPDATED: Azure outage affecting Windows Updates and related services
items.content_text: UPDATED 1:45 PM PST : Resolution efforts are complete at this time, and Windows services are expected to be back to normal. Notices related to this issue have been moved to history sections across Azure status pages. At this time, Windows Update is expected to update and download as normal, and functionality has been restored across Microsoft Update Catalog, Microsoft Store, and Windows Server Update Services (WSUS).

items.title: Outage affecting Windows Updates and related services
items.content_text: An Azure outage is presently affecting several Windows services. Impacted services include Windows Update and the Microsoft Update Catalog. Users might also experience issues accessing or downloading content from the Microsoft Store and Windows Server Update Services (WSUS). Users might encounter delays, failures, or timeouts with downloads or access to these services. Additional related services might also be affected.

items.title: 90-Day Reminder: Windows 11, version 24H2 and Windows 10 LTSB 2016 reaching end of updates on October 13, 2026
items.content_text: On October 13, 2026, Windows 11, version 24H2 Home and Pro editions , and Windows 10 Enterprise LTSB 2016 will reach end of updates. After this date, devices running these editions will no longer receive monthly security and non-security preview updates containing protections from the latest security threats.

items.title: Updated guidance explains Windows monthly updates and servicing
items.content_text: The recently updated Understanding Windows monthly updates: Servicing explained blog provides a clear overview of how Windows quality updates are released, including the differences between monthly security updates, non-security preview updates, out-of-band (OOB) updates, and new features and improvements. It also explains how these updates work together to help keep devices secure and supported.

items.title: Enforcement phase for Kerberos RC4 protections begins with the July 2026 Windows security update
items.content_text: The July 2026 Windows security update enables Enforcement mode across Windows domain controllers for Kerberos RC4 protections related to CVE‑2026‑20833 . Domain controllers enforce updated behavior for service account ticket issuance. RC4-based Kerberos tickets are restricted under this enforcement model, and AES-based encryption is expected for supported configurations. items.title: Windows settings backup policy is becoming a new default
items.content_text: Starting with Windows 11, version 26H2, the default behavior of the Windows settings backup policy will shift from disabled to enabled. Default-on applies only to eligible devices and only when admins haven't explicitly set the policy. Explicit enablement and disablement settings are always honored. Restore behavior is unchanged: it remains admin-controlled and isn’t enabled by default.

items.title: Point-in-time restore for Windows is generally available
items.content_text: Point-in-time restore is a new, built-in Windows recovery feature that lets users roll their Windows PC back to a previous state in minutes. In Windows Recovery Environment (WinRE), users can select a daily restore point to roll back to. Restore points include the Windows OS, installed applications, system and app configurations, settings, and local user files. items.title: Configuring firewall and proxies for smooth Windows updates
items.content_text: If you’re an IT admin, a new guide is here to help you troubleshoot connection issues with the Windows Update service. Some of these issues have to do with the embedded network security design. The key is in the configuration of your network endpoints for firewalls and proxies. Essentially, make sure to trust Fully Qualified Domain Name (FQDN) subdomains of the recommended DNS subdomains. Dive deeper at Configuring firewall and proxies for smooth Windows updates .

items.title: Remote Desktop adds new safeguards for connections opened from RDP files
items.content_text: Starting with the April 2026 Windows security update, Remote Desktop adds new safeguards to help reduce phishing attacks that misuse Remote Desktop (.rdp) files. These attacks attempt to trick devices into connecting to attacker‑controlled systems and sharing local resources without clear visibility. To address this, a new security dialog appears before any connection is made, showing the remote computer address, publisher information (when available), and any requested access to local resources

items.content_text: Windows updates released in April 2026 and later begin the second deployment phase of protections designed to address a Kerberos information disclosure vulnerability ( CVE‑2026‑20833 ). This second phase continues the shift away from legacy encryption types such as RC4 by moving toward stronger default ticket behavior. items.title: Windows Deployment Services (WDS): Hands-free deployment hardening (Phase 2)
items.content_text: As announced in January 2026, the Unattend.xml file used in hands‑free deployment poses a vulnerability when transmitted over an unauthenticated RPC channel. Beginning with the April 2026 security update, the second phase of hardening changes for CVE-2026-0386 is now in effect. These changes make hands‑free deployment disabled by default to enforce secure behavior.

items.title: Hardening administrative actions: Windows imaging, cloning, and auth workflows
items.content_text: Administrative actions are undergoing hardening changes that might require operational change to support your organization’s security posture. With the August 2025 Windows non-security update, devices were hardened against unauthorized attempts to bypass loopback detection. However, if you’ve cloned machines without Sysprep, you might see Kerberos and NTLM authentication failures. This is by design. The recommended solution is to rebuild affected devices using supported imaging methods. A tempor

items.title: Extended Security Updates (ESU) available for Windows 10 Enterprise LTSB 2016
items.content_text: If your organization relies on Windows 10 Enterprise LTSB 2016, you know it’s nearing end of support on October 13, 2026. While the long-term solution is upgrading to a newer LTSC release, another temporary solution is also now available. Extended Security Updates (ESU) are now available for purchase through Volume Licensing or a Microsoft Cloud Solution Provider (CSP). Learn more about this transition at Plan for Windows Server 2016 and Windows 10 2016 LTSB end of support .

items.title: ESR management is moving to Windows Backup for Organizations
items.content_text: If your IT department relies on Enterprise State Roaming (ESR) to synchronize user settings, plan for change. Starting May 2026, ESR management is moving to Windows Backup for Organizations. Previously, IT admins were able to access it from the Microsoft Entra portal. This old option will be still available until the end of June 2026. Please start accessing the new default experience through Windows Backup for Organizations policies . Learn more here .

By shifting update management from reactive to proactive, it helps improve update success rates and strengthen overall fleet reliability.

items.title: Refreshing the root of trust: industry collaboration on Secure Boot certificate updates
items.content_text: Secure Boot is a foundational Windows security feature that runs at startup, before Windows load, and helps ensure that only trusted, digitally signed software can execute. items.title: Advancing Windows security: Disabling NTLM by default
items.content_text: Windows is moving toward a more secure authentication model by phasing out New Technology LAN Manager (NTLM) in favor of stronger, Kerberos‑based alternatives. This transition is taking a three-phased approach, leading toward disabling NTLM by default in upcoming Windows releases. With each phase come new capabilities so that your organization has the tools, visibility, and compatibility support needed. Learn more at Advancing Windows security: Disabling NTLM by default.

items.title: Hardening changes coming to Common Log File System (CLFS) authentication
items.content_text: Windows 11, version 25H2 and Windows Server 2025 updates released on or after October 28, 2025 include a new hardening authentication mitigation for the Common Log File System (CLFS) driver. This change adds a hash-based message authentication code (HMAC) to the underlying files of a CLFS logfile. Authentication codes are generated by combining file data with a system-unique cryptographic key stored in the registry, accessible only to administrators and SYSTEM accounts. items.title: Version 2507: August 12
items.content_text: Version 2507 (Build 19029.20184) Security updates listed here . Resolved issues Outlook We fixed an issue that caused screen readers to fail to announce error messages when using Draft with Copilot. We fixed an issue that caused Outlook to display the Prioritize and Settings buttons when they shouldn't appear, such as when the user doesn't have a Copilot license or has disabled the Copilot UI.

items.title: Outlook (new) release notes: August 8, 2025
items.content_text: Version: 20250801002.10 Find text in reading pane: use the familiar shortcut Ctrl + F to find text in the reading pane. Category support for Google, Yahoo, IMAP, and POP accounts: extended support for categories in mail and calendar to more account types. New PDF previewer - improvements to the way PDF attachments open.

items.title: Version 2507: August 05
items.content_text: Version 2507 (Build 19029.20156) Resolved issues Outlook We fixed an issue that required users to restart Outlook to open an .msg file after initially accessing it once.

items.title: Outlook (new) release notes: August 1, 2025
items.content_text: Version: 20250725003.04 New RSVP settings: Two new settings to improve the user experience when responding to meeting invitations: (1) don't delete invitation email after RSVPing and (2) autodecline if the attendee's calendar shows as busy. Agents in Copilot Chat: Support for declarative agents in Copilot chat in Outlook, already rolled out to Microsoft 365 Copilot chat.

items.title: Version 2507: July 30
items.content_text: Version 2507 (Build 19029.20136) Resolved issues Outlook We fixed an issue where Outlook may close unexpectedly after launch. Word Resolved an issue where URLs pasted from Outlook may be broken when the setting "Internet and network paths with hyperlinks" is turned off. Resolved an issue where Word may close unexpectedly when exporting a specific document as a PDF. Resolved an issue in Word where selecting Save Selection to Text Box Gallery may cause some characters to display incorrectly as squ

items.title: Version 2506: July 22
items.content_text: Version 2506 (Build 18925.20184) Resolved issues Outlook We fixed an issue that caused the Copilot Command Center to continue to be visible after disabling the Copilot user interface. PowerPoint We fixed an issue when creating handouts in PowerPoint, certain characters (full-width numbers) can't be properly transferred to the handout.
